data.title: Eli Lilly Vs. Hims & Hers In DTC Weight Loss Market? - There Is Only One Winner
data.description:
Investment Overview

Eli Lilly and Company (NYSE:LLY) is the world's largest Pharmaceutical company by market cap, and it is worth nearly twice as much as the next largest, ~$768bn, versus the ~$370bn valuation of Johnson & Johnson (JNJ). Lilly is only the eight largest Pharma by revenues in 2024 – $45bn, earning nearly half of JNJ's ~$89bn.

The reason Lilly is worth so much in the market's eyes is down to one drug molecule, tirzepatide, a glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) mimetic, which is marketed and sold as Zepbound in the indication of weight loss, and as Mounjaro in the indication of Type 2 Diabetes.

Zepbound earned $4.93bn in 2024, its first full year on the market, and Mounjaro, approved in 2022, $11.54bn. Readers will likely be aware what the defining characteristic of tirzepatide is – it has the ability to induce rapid weight-loss in patients that the scientific community. The latest clinical data shared by Lilly indicated a mean weight loss of 20.2% at 72 weeks – 47% greater than its biggest competitor, Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Wegovy (semaglutide), which targets glucagon like peptide-1 only.

Because these drugs are relatively new to market and complicated to manufacture, last year, the FDA declared a global shortage of both tirzepatide and semaglutide, meaning that compounded drug manufacturers were permitted to manufacture the drug product also. According to the FDA:

Compounded drugs are not FDA-approved. This means that FDA does not verify the safety, effectiveness or quality of compounded drugs before they are marketed. Consumers and health care professionals rely on FDA’s drug approval process for verification of safety, effectiveness and quality.

Into the breach stepped Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS), an enterprising "consumer-first health and wellness platform focused on providing access to modern personalized health and wellness solutions to consumers" (source: Hims 2024 Annual Report / 10-K submission).

Best known for selling "general wellness, skincare, sexual health and wellness, and hair care" products via its DTC platform, which offers subscription services and access to telehealth consultations and prescriptions, Hims partnered with compounded drug manufacturers and began offering compounded semaglutide via its platform, which was permitted so long as semaglutide remained on the global drug shortage list.

This created both a media frenzy and a huge increase in platform visits and subscribers to Hims' platform – in 2024, subscriptions grew 45% to 2.2m. Hims' share price made astonishing gains – in January 2024, shares traded ~$8.5, by February 2025 they were worth >$60 per share.

Naturally, neither Novo Nordisk nor Eli Lilly wanted any compounded semaglutide / tirzepatide being sold by any company, and both have invested tens of billions of dollars in new manufacturing capabilities – Novo Nordisk moving to acquire Catalent, a drug manufacturer, and Lilly promising to invest $27bn in four new manufacturing plants.

In February, the FDA declared that the semaglutide shortage was over. Tirzepatide was removed from the list in October, before being reinstated, after compounded drug manufacturers filed a lawsuit against the agency, before removing it again in December. That means that drug compounders will have to stop manufacturing either drug – not good news for Hims, whose share price has quickly retreated to <$30.

What Now For Hims? A Chance To Take On Eli Lilly In DTC Market?

In its annual report, Hims states that:

In 2024, we acquired Seaview Enterprises, LLC (d/b/a MedisourceRx) (“MedisourceRx”), a licensed 503B compounding outsourcing facility, which compounds products primarily for Hims & Hers customers (our “Outsourcing Facility”).

The likelihood seems to be that this facility will not be manufacturing tirzepatide or semaglutide any time soon – in addition to no longer being on the shortage list, Hims says in its 2024 10-K:

Further, in 2024, the manufacturers of certain FDA-approved GLP-1 products requested FDA add semaglutide and tirzepatide to its “Demonstrable Difficulties for Compounding List”. FDA has never finalized the Demonstrable Difficulties for Compounding List for any drug products, but if FDA were to add semaglutide to, and finalize, the list, we could no longer compound these products.

Tirzepatide was never available from Hims's platform, although doubtless Hims would have offered it in time had it remained on the shortage list, despite the fact Lilly insists it is a harder drug to manufacture than semaglutide.

Meanwhile, Lilly has taken steps to develop its own Direct-to-Consumer ("DTC") platform to make it easier for patients to buy tirzpepatide online. LillyDirect is a telehealth platform that is owned by Lilly; however, its function is apparently to pass patients through to other companies, e.g., Form Health, who will evaluate patients and make prescriptions as necessary, thereby circumventing any conflicts of interest. Form will not receive any financial compensation from Lilly for prescribing the drug.

According to Reuters, another primary care provider, Knownwell, also joined the platform this week, while Lilly itself says the platform has been expanded to offer its Alzheimer's drug, Kisunla. According to a blog post, Hims and Hers planned to offer compounded tirzepatide "starting at $200 per month."

No wonder Lilly and Novo were so keen to close that loophole, but seemingly, Hims has concluded "if you can't beat them, join them." The company announced yesterday that added the branded versions of tirzepatide to its platform – in a blog post released on April 1st (presumably not an April fool), the company's Senior Vice President for weight management:

We’re committed to bringing our customers more treatment options that best suit their needs, and we’ve now expanded that choice even further by adding access to generic liraglutide and branded tirzepatide through our platform. This is in addition to oral medication kits, compounded semaglutide, and branded semaglutide.

It's interesting the company still mentions compounded semaglutide, as the FDA has indicated it will begin taking action against compounders for violations of its policy within 60–90 days of removing them from the shortage list. Liraglutide, meanwhile, is a GLP-1 drug developed by Novo Nordisk, that lacks the weight loss qualities of semaglutide or tirzepatide.

Will Hims' Latest Ploy Work - And Will Lilly Lend Its Support?

There appears to be one major drawback to HIMS' latest strategy, which apparently, comes with no endorsement from Lilly, which has been quoted as saying "Lilly has no affiliation with Hims & Hers. Zepbound can be prescribed by any licensed healthcare professional."

The issue is price. Lilly is offering a single dose vial of tirzepatide via its DTC platform for $349 per month at the 2.5mg dose, and $499 at the 5mg dose, while the single dose pen is available from $25 per month, with doses ranging from 2.5mg to 15mg. Lilly says in a statement:

Every major medical organization and establishment recognizes obesity as a chronic disease, yet insurance and federal programs do not systematically cover people living with obesity for medical care — this needs to change," said Patrik Jonsson, executive vice president and president of Lilly Cardiometabolic Health and Lilly USA.

"Lilly is committed to working with all parties to solve this problem, and in the meantime, we'll continue to implement new options that improve the affordability and availability of our safe, approved and studied Zepbound for patients who are being asked to pay out-of-pocket."

Hims, meanwhile, is apparently offering Zepbound and Mounjaro for $1,899 per month, with generic liraglutide available at $299 per month. That would appear to be a major stumbling block, as consumers won't want to pay such a high fee for tirzepatide when it is available for a fraction of the price via DTC, or potentially even less for those who have insurance, while liraglutide is a demonstrably inferior product.

Implications For Lilly and Hims Share Prices Going Forward?

At present, Lilly trades at a forward price to sales ratio of close to 13x, based on management's forecast for ~$60bn revenues in 2025, and a price to earnings ratio of ~70, while Hims trades at a P/S of ~2.75, based on forecasting ~$2.35bn of revenues in 2025, and a price to earnings of ~58x.

Lilly's valuation has risen so high that it seems a correction must be due at some juncture. However, I have said this before when Lilly was worth $400bn, $500bn, $600bn and so on – this is completely uncharted territory for a Pharma company, and at present I do believe the company can achieve the magical $1 trillion valuation mark, as I stated in a note covering Lilly's earnings in February. The Pharma is still scratching the surface when it comes to the number of new markets it may be able to target with tirzepatide.

Hims' share price is already down by ~50% since early February, so there is a case to argue that the drug shortage / compounding setbacks are already priced into the valuation. The question is, will Hims' subscribers now begin to depart the platform en masse, now there is no compounded semaglutide available at <$300 per month?

In January 2024, prior to any of these developments, Hims stock was worth <$10 per share, so down nearly 65% on today's traded price. I suspect we may be heading back in that direction, now that the compounding loophole has been closed, likely, in the case of semaglutide and tirzepatide, permanently.

In summary, in my opinion, recent developments heavily favor Lilly, the 800 pound gorilla of the weight loss industry, which can afford to spend $27bn on manufacturing, given weight loss is expected to be a triple-digit billion market before 2030.

HIMS is by no means a busted flush, and its P/S metric is attractive, and customers may still end up buying liraglutide or $1,800 tirzepatide for some time, being ignorant of the lower prices, but this is not a sustainable market, and I wonder if the company will be providing lower annual revenue guidance for 2026 than it did for 2025. It would not surprise me if that proves the case.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Update On Oil Services And The OIH ETF Product (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Petroleum prices were going nowhere fast over the past weeks, with the nearby NYMEX crude oil futures (CL1:COM) trading below $70 per barrel since early March and Brent futures (CO1:COM) trading just over the level until the risk-off price action on April 3 caused oil prices to drop to the bottom end of its trading range. Crude oil remains the energy commodity powering the world, but bullish and bearish factors pull the price in opposite directions.

While seasonality suggests that crude oil prices could increase during the 2025 peak driving season during spring and summer, OPEC+ announced it will increase production in April. Meanwhile, the U.S. administration has pledged to “drill-baby-drill” to increase output, reduce prices and inflation, create energy independence, increase exports and revenues, and limit OPEC+’s pricing power.

The VanEck Oil Services ETF (NYSEARCA:OIH) product owns shares in leading multinational companies providing services to the oil industry.

On December 10, 2024, Seeking Alpha article on OIH, I concluded:

I favor buying VanEck Oil Services ETF on any significant price corrections over the coming months, as the oil service companies will support the Trump administration’s plans to “drill-baby-drill” and “frack-baby-frack.” At $288 per share, the ETF is in the middle of the trading range and could move lower if crude oil prices sink in 2025. Approaching OIH at lower prices will require a strategy with plenty of room to add on further declines. As the market learned in March and April 2020, oil service stocks can be highly volatile, earning the ETF its failing grade in risk. At the current price, I rate OIH a hold but will look to add on declines on a scaled-down basis.

OIH was trading at the $288.16 per share level on December 10. It was significantly lower on April 3, at around $240. While I am bearish on crude oil prices, increasing production in the U.S. and Middle East could increase oil services demand and support earnings for the oil services ETF product.

Oil prices remain in a bearish trend

After reaching $130.50 per barrel when Russia invaded Ukraine in March 2022, NYMEX WTI crude oil futures prices have made lower highs, falling 51.3% to a $63.57 low in May 2023. While the energy commodity has not made a lower low in nearly two years, the price remains not far above the low, which is the energy commodity’s critical technical support.

The monthly continuous contract chart highlights the decline and the $65.22 to $79.39 per barrel trading range in 2025. While the long-term chart reflects a sideways trading pattern, the short-term trend remains bearish.

The daily chart illustrates the pattern of lower highs and lower lows from January 15, 2025, through the March 5, 2025, low. May NYMEX crude oil futures reached a $65.22 per barrel low on March 5 but recovered near the $70 level in early April before giving up all the gains on April 3. The recovery has remained below the first technical resistance level at the February 20 $72.90 per barrel high. After taking the stairs higher, crude oil futures took an elevator shaft to the downside on April 3.

The U.S. administration wants lower crude oil prices for four significant reasons

U.S. President Trump has pledged to lower consumer oil and oil product prices. Calling crude oil the abundant “liquid gold” in the U.S., his administration has four compelling reasons for pushing oil prices lower:

  • Even though core inflation data excludes volatile food and energy prices, crude oil remains the energy commodity that powers the world. Most goods and services move higher and lower with oil and other traditional energy prices. Lower prices will reduce inflationary pressures.
  • Lower crude oil prices and more U.S. production will achieve energy independence from OPEC+, the petroleum cartel. Reducing the cartel’s pricing dominance will yield geopolitical benefits.
  • “Drill baby drill” U.S. energy policy that shifts the U.S. from a net importer to an exporter will increase revenues, lowering the trade deficits and national debt.
  • President Trump pledged to keep his promises, and lower oil and energy prices were high on his list.

Crude oil prices on the nearby NYMEX contract during the first Trump administration ranged from $6.50 to $76.90 per barrel. During the Biden administration, the range was from $63.57 to $130.50 per barrel.

The wars in the Middle East and Ukraine could be ending - Bearish for crude oil

The administration is working to mediate solutions to the ongoing wars in Gaza and Ukraine and has warned Iran of the severe consequences of developing a nuclear weapon. If President Trump succeeds, peaceful resolutions will remove some of the crude oil’s geopolitical premium, which has kept prices elevated. Given his pledge to lower petroleum prices, oil production policy in the Middle East and Russia are likely negotiating topics to settle the ongoing conflicts.

The bottom line is that peace in Europe and the Middle East would likely cause oil prices to decline below the technical support level at $63.57 per barrel and head toward the $50 level.

Meanwhile, OPEC+ increased output in April, responding to improvement in China’s economy and the shift in U.S. energy policy from support for alternative and renewable fuels to “drill-baby-drill” and “frack-baby-frack.”

The reasons that oil services companies could profit

Increasing oil production means the demand for oil services will rise. Even if oil prices decline, profits for oil-related companies that service wells, drilling platforms, and other necessities for extracting energy commodities from the earth’s crust will rise.

A December 2024 Reuters article highlighted the potential for increased M&A activity in the oilfield service sector under the Trump administration. John England, Deloitte’s global sector leader for oil, gas, and chemicals, said:

We think the new administration could be positive for M&A, and that we will see a little more loosening around that because it was getting more difficult to get M&A done the last few years. A fairly fragmented (oilfield service) market and some loosening from the administration sets a nice stage for potential consolidation.

Consolidation in oil services could mean premiums for smaller companies and accretive acquisitions for the leaders.

OIH is the oil services ETF product

The most recent top holdings of the VanEck Oil Services ETF (OIH) product were:

OIH has a 39.28% exposure to the top three oil services companies: Schlumberger Limited (SLB), Baker Hughes Company (BKR), and Halliburton Company (HAL). Since my December 10 Seeking Alpha piece on OIH, the ETF slightly decreased exposure to BKR and HAL, while exposure to SLB was virtually unchanged. The overall allocation to the three leaders decreased from 41.95%.

At around $241 per share, OIH had $1.23 billion in assets under management. OIH trades over 417,000 shares daily and charges a 0.35% management fee. The annual blended $5.44 dividend translates to a 2.26% yield.

The seven-year monthly chart highlights the over 450% rise from the March 2020 pandemic-inspired $66 low to the September 2023 $364.08 high, at which point the oil services ETF ran out of upside steam.

The first technical support level was at the May 2023 low of $246.04, and OIH eclipsed that level in March 2025, trading to a $241.01 low before recovering to over the $260 level in early April. On April 3, OIH made a slightly lower low at the $239.66 level.

OIH’s bearish price action results in a failing grade in risk and a D- in the momentum categories. The expense ratio at 0.35% earns a B, while dividends and liquidity receive A grades.

If the demand for oil services increases as the U.S. returns to a “drill-baby-drill” and “frack-baby-frack” energy policy and fewer regulations ignite an M&A wave, we could see oil services stocks and the OIH surprise on the upside. I remain a buyer of the oil services ETF at the current price level.

Demystify commodities in an era of uncertainty

Commodities experienced significant volatility in 2024, with energy prices impacted by geopolitical tensions, while precious metals and industrial metals saw significant gains. Looking ahead to 2025, the outlook remains murky, with trade policies shaping future commodity performance. During these times, you need someone who can share their knowledge and expertise to position for success.

The Hecht Commodity Report, led by seasoned Wall Street veteran Andrew Hecht, offers in-depth, actionable insights into everything from oil, gold, and grains to currencies and industrial metals. Subscribers receive weekly commentary, trade alerts, and forward-looking strategies and a proven approach that has helped investors navigate complex markets for decades, providing clear, actionable buy and sell recommendations.

Start your risk-free trial today and lock in the current price before it increases to $999 on April 30, 2025.

data.title: Bank of America: Series L Preferred Shares A Hold After Recent Outperformance
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past year, Bank of America (BAC)'s Series L convertible non-callable 7.25% fixed-rate preferred shares (NYSE:BAC.PR.L) has outperformed the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF), delivering a total return of ~8%:

In this article, I present three valuation scenarios for the Series L preferred shares, with returns ranging from -14% to +9.5%, with a base case scenario of +5.6% capital gain over the next three years. I conclude that the recent outperformance has somewhat reduced the capital appreciation potential and rank the Series L shares a Hold.

Company Overview

You can access all BAC results on its website here. Bank of America reports results in four business segments - Consumer Banking at 38% of 2024 revenue, Global Wealth & Investment Management at 21%, Global Banking at 22%, and Global Markets at 20%, with the remaining results booked in All Other (totals do not add to 100% due to rounding).

In 2024, 55% of Bank of America's revenue came from net interest income, with the remaining 45% derived from noninterest income. The 45% of revenue coming from non-interest sources is relatively high (especially compared to smaller lenders), making Bank of America less exposed to lower interest rates as the Fed looks to continue with its policy normalization in the years ahead.

Safety of the preferred shares

At the end of 2024, Bank of America had $23.2 billion in cumulative preferred shares outstanding (at par value). These preferred shares are covered by common equity of ~$300 billion roughly 13x (at market value), indicating a very high degree of safety.

In 2024, Bank of America paid $1.6 billion in dividends to preferred shareholders, representing just 6% of net income before preferred distributions, again underscoring the high safety profile of preferred dividends.

Bank of America is set to report Q1 2025 earnings on April 15. Analysts expect EPS of $0.82/share, up almost 11% year-over-year. As the U.S. economic outlook has deteriorated recently, looking at BAC's macroeconomic assumptions can help us gauge whether a precautionary reserve build should be expected in Q1 2025.

Bank of America's provisioning is quite conservative going into 2025, with the bank expecting unemployment of 4.8% this year and GDP growth of just 1.4%:

Putting the numbers from the above snippet into context, they are lower than the Fed's own forecasts released in March 2025, which saw U.S. GDP growth at 1.7% and unemployment of 4.4% in 2025. As such, I do not expect a large precautionary reserve build going into Q1 2025 earnings. This should allow Bank of America to meet analyst estimates and maintain the good coverage of preferred dividends.

Historical yields

Details for all BAC preferred shares, including prospectuses, are available here. The Series L 7.25% fixed-rate non-cumulative convertible preferred shares were issued back in January 2008. Each convertible share can be converted into twenty common shares.

As the common stock currently trades well below levels that would entice convertible shareholders to exercise their conversion rights, the pricing of the convertible preferred shares primarily depends on BAC's credit risk and risk-free interest rates. Still, it is worth mentioning that in the next 10–20 years, investors may be able to convert their shares at a profit. As such, I think investors buying the Series L shares now are getting a "free" call option on BAC stock in the distant future.

Seeking Alpha provides pricing data on the Series L shares going back to 2008. As you might expect, the Series L shares crashed during the 2008 financial crisis, reaching an all-time low of $290/share in February 2009. A swift recovery ensued, and outside of a brief period in 2011 (the European debt crisis), they have never traded below the par value of $1000.

My analysis will focus on the last five years, covering the pricing of the Series L preferred shares immediately before the COVID-19 pandemic, during the COVID-19 2020 crash, the five-year share price low in October 2023, and the one-year high reached in September 2024.

In the table below, I have summarized the historical yields of the Series L preferred shares during the select time periods:

Period Price, $ share Series L Yield
Late 2019-Early 2020 ~1500 ~4.8%
COVID-19 March 2020 Crash ~1172 ~6.2%
October 2023 ~1050 ~6.9%
September 2024 ~1290 ~5.60%
April 2025 ~1210 ~6%

Source: Author calculations

Yield spreads relative to risk-free interest rates

As highlighted above, BAC's cumulative preferred shares are covered by common equity market capitalization 13x, while preferred dividends only accounted for 6% of 2024 net income before preferred distributions. As such, a principal driver behind the movement in the Series L preferred share price are changes in risk-free interest rates, both on the short end (the Fed funds rate) as well as the longer end (10-year U.S. treasury notes).

In the table below, I have compiled the monthly Federal Funds Effective Rate and yields on United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) for the periods highlighted in the previous paragraph:

Period Fed Funds Effective Rate /A/ 10-year U.S. Treasury Note /B/ Series L Yield /C/ Yield Spread (C minus A and B)
Late 2019-Early 2020 ~1.5% ~1.9% ~4.8% ~2.9-3.3%
COVID-19 March 2020 Crash ~0.65% ~0.7% ~6.2% ~5.5%
October 2023 ~5.33% ~4.80% ~6.9% 1.6-2.1%
September 2024 ~5.13% ~3.70% ~5.60% 0.5%-1.9%
April 2025 ~4.33% ~4.05% ~6% 1.7-2%

Source: Author calculations

I think these yield spreads primarily reflect the chance of Bank of America defaulting on its preferred obligations, as well as the lower liquidity in the Series L preferred shares.

Looking at the table above, we see that yield spreads of the Series L preferred shares were roughly 2.9-3.3% before COVID-19 struck the economy. At the peak of the COVID-19 market crash, spreads were at their peak of about 5.5%.

More recently, high risk-free interest rates in October 2023 resulted in narrowing yield spreads of only 1.6-2.1%. The one-year high in the preferred share price in September 2024 coincided with the beginning of the Fed rate-cutting cycle, marking an absolute low in yield spreads relative to short-term rates.

The principal conclusions we can draw are that spreads were higher before the COVID-19 pandemic, peaked during the financial panic of March 2020, and have subsequently moved lower to about 2% relative to 10-year U.S. risk-free interest rates.

Putting spreads into context

If you take the late 2019-early 2020 spreads at face value, you may think the Series L preferred shares are overvalued. For instance, applying a similar spread relative to current risk-free interest rates would imply a fair value of the Series L preferred shares marginally above par value of $1000/share (about $1030/share), implying a 14% downside from the current share price of $1210/share.

What is important to remember is that back then, the outlook for interest rates was higher, with the Fed expecting a Fed funds rate of 2.5% in its December 2019 summary of economic projections. Fast-forward to 2025, while there is significant uncertainty as to the pace of Fed policy normalization, the central bank still expects to lower interest rates by roughly 0.5% annually in 2025-2027, ultimately reaching a neutral rate of 3%. Likewise, the Congressional Budget Office expects 10-year yields to decline marginally to roughly 3.8% in the long term.

Applying a ~2.3% yield spread to the 3.00-3.8% risk-free interest rate expectations, we see a fair value yield for the Series L preferred shares of 5.3-6.1%. The 2.3% yield spread is consistent with the expected yield premium before COVID-19, and marginally higher than that observed over the past two years. It would imply a fair value for the preferred shares of $1188-$1367/share, or about $1278 at the midpoint. This represents a capital appreciation potential of about 5.6% and would put the Series L share price close to the highs reached in September 2024.

All in all, we see that upside potential in the Series L shares largely depends on the Fed cutting interest rates on the short end of the yield curve. While I do expect the Fed to continue with rate cuts, my fair value estimate only shows capital appreciation upside of 5.6%, which warrants a Hold rating for the Series L preferred shares. Of course, you may arrive at a higher fair value estimate if you reduce the 2.3% yield premium closer to 2.1% (as the one I relied on in my JPMorgan Chase (JPM) article), as shown in the table below:

Scenario Fair Value, $ Share Price Change (at midpoint) Explanation
Pessimistic ~1030 -14% Return to yield spreads before COVID-19
Base Case 1188-1367 5.6% Yield spread of 2.3% in the long term
Optimistic 1229-1422 9.5% Yield spread of 2.1% in the long term

Source: Author calculations

Risks

While Bank of America's preferred shares are well covered with common equity and their dividends represent only account for 6% of 2024 net income, I think investing in the preferred shares faces several principal risks:

  • Upside capital appreciation largely depends on the Fed lowering interest rates, which is uncertain in timing and magnitude.
  • The preferred shares trade above par value of $1000, somewhat reducing their safety (I should highlight that they are not callable at par, i.e. the risk is limited to BAC defaulting on preferred payments).
  • As shown in the pessimistic scenario, yield spreads may return to pre-COVID-19 levels, resulting in capital losses.

Conclusion

Bank of America's Series L preferred shares have delivered strong returns over the past year, outperforming the PFF ETF. As their distributions are well covered by BAC's net income and equity market capitalization, my base case shows that investors can reasonably expect to continue collecting a 6% yield while also benefiting from a 5.6% capital gain once the Fed is done normalizing policy.

I rank the Series L preferred shares a Hold as the capital appreciation upside is quite limited and the potential to take advantage of the conversion option is arguably decades into the future. That said, investors who ascribe a lower required rate of return may find the current value of the Series L preferred shares appealing, as the price is quite sensitive to small changes in valuation assumptions.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Lowe's Companies: The Picture Is Improving (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have never enjoyed downgrading companies. In an ideal world, every stock would justify a bullish outlook. But we don't live in such a world. You can imagine my displeasure, then, when in November of last year I ended up downgrading home improvement retailer Lowe's Companies, Inc. (NYSE:LOW). At the time, the business was experiencing weakness on both its top and bottom lines. There was also uncertainty regarding what the future would hold. At the end of the day, I believed that a more cautious approach was appropriate. Honestly, since then, things have gone even worse for the company than I thought they would have. Shares are currently down 13.1% at a time when the S&P 500 (SP500) is down only 3.1%.

Fundamentally speaking, some weakness for the company has continued as of late. However, there does appear to be a reason for optimism. Management expects revenue growth this year, though profitability probably will be a bit lower than it was in 2024. Shares have gotten cheaper on an absolute basis and do look cheap compared to similar companies. This is most certainly encouraging. Management continues to return significant amounts of capital to shareholders and while margins are not the highest for the industry, they aren't far off. Given this combination of factors, I believe that upgrading it once again to a soft “buy” is appropriate.

This prospect makes sense now

The most recent fundamental data that we have regarding Lowe's Companies covers the final quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. Revenue for that time came in at $18.55 billion. That's a small drop from the $18.60 billion reported the previous year. Even though revenue dropped year over year, the company benefited from a rise in the number of locations in operation from 1,746 to 1,748. Comparable store sales inched up by 0.2% year over year, with the company benefiting from a rise in the average transaction value from $101.45 to $101.80. The weakness for the business can be attributed to a decline in the number of transactions from 180 million to 177 million.

According to management, the company benefited during this time from high single digit Pro sales growth. Online comparable sales increased, and the business benefited from both a strong holiday season and rebuilding efforts associated with the hurricanes that ravaged parts of the U.S. late last year. The weakness for the company involved do-it-yourself discretionary spending. This is not surprising to me in the least. As I detailed in a different article, inflationary pressures in recent years, combined with high interest rates aimed at combating inflation, negatively affected the ability and/or willingness of consumers to spend on things like repair and remodeling activities.

The housing industry has also been faced with similar challenges. On a trailing 12-month basis as of the end of the 2024 fiscal year, total home improvements and repairs spending in the U.S. came in at $503 billion. That was down from the $510 billion reported one year earlier. Furthermore, in 2024, total housing starts were 1.36 million units. That was down from the 1.42 million reported the previous year. This was driven by a 25% plunge in multifamily properties that more than offset a 6.5% increase in single family home building.

On the bottom line, the picture for the business did improve slightly. Net income grew from $1.02 billion to $1.13 billion. Other profitability metrics ended up being mixed. For instance, operating cash flow took quite a tumble, dropping from $1.11 billion to $911 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, however, we would get only a marginal fall from $1.76 billion to $1.75 billion. And finally, we have EBITDA. It managed to rise from $2.18 billion to $2.42 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2024 fiscal year compared to 2023. Revenue during this time tumbled by 3.1% from $86.38 billion to $83.67 billion. During the year, the company saw a 2.7% drop in comparable store sales. This was driven by a 3% fall in the number of transactions from 835 million to 809 million. This was also accompanied by a decline in the average ticket from $103.51 to $103.37. But on a comparable basis, the average ticket grew 0.3%. Management blamed this on the aforementioned factors involving inflation and high interest rates.

The decline in revenue brought with it a drop in profits as well. Net income dropped from $7.73 billion to $6.96 billion. On top of suffering from a decline in sales, the company also saw rising costs. Primarily, I am referring to selling, general, and administrative expenses. These grew from 18.02% of sales to 18.74%. This might not seem like all that much. But when applied to the revenue the company generated last year, it translates to $602.5 million that the business missed out on when it came to pre-tax profits. Other profitability metrics ended up being mixed. Operating cash flow jumped from $8.14 billion to $9.63 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $10.37 billion to $9.51 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company contracted from $13.63 billion to $12.48 billion.

Even though these results look disappointing, 2025 is looking up from a sales perspective. Management currently believes that revenue will total between $83.5 billion and $84.5 billion. The midpoint here would translate to a year-over-year increase of 0.4%, with comparable sales ranging between being flat and rising by 1%. On the bottom line, the picture will be a little less attractive. Earnings per share are expected to be between $12.15 and $12.40. At the midpoint, this would be roughly $6.90 billion, which would be about $58 million lower than what the company achieved last year. However, it is worth noting that this could change based on the decisions that the company makes regarding share buybacks.

Speaking of share buybacks, Lowe's Companies has a long history of returning capital to shareholders in this way. In 2024, the company returned $6.5 billion to investors in the form of dividends and buybacks. $3.93 billion of this was in the form of share repurchases. During the year, the company acquired 15.8 million shares. In the chart above, you can see how this is dwarfed by share buybacks for 2022 and 2023. But still, over this three-year window of time, the company repurchased a whopping 116.9 million shares at a cost of $24.39 billion. In all likelihood, this trend will continue this year as well.

If we use the historical results that we have gotten for the 2023 and 2024 fiscal years, we can see in the chart above how the company is priced on a price to earnings basis, on a price to adjusted operating cash flow basis, and on an EV to EBITDA basis. On its own, these are multiples that I would consider to be more or less fairly valued. However, this is a major industry player that I consider to be incredibly safe. This certainly justifies some flexibility in an assessment. The chart also compares the company to two similar firms. This includes the behemoth that is The Home Depot (HD).

Relative to these other players, Lowe's Companies certainly looks cheap. There are other ways value these companies. In the next chart, shown above, you can see how the businesses look from a price to sales perspective and from an EV to EBITDA sales perspective. Once again, Lowe's Companies is cheaper than these other firms.

To be honest with you, I don't really see a good reason why this is. I had prepared to look at revenue and profit growth of these companies over the last five years as part of this analysis. However, this data would paint a picture that's not comparable for Lowe's Companies because of its decision to divest itself of its Canadian operations. This transaction occurred in February 2023 and involved the sale of 232 stores that it operated, and 210 dealer owned stores that it serviced, spread throughout Canada. That business brought in $5.05 billion in revenue in its final fiscal year in 2022.

Instead, there are other ways to look at these enterprises. For starters, in the chart above, you can see the net leverage ratio of each of these firms. Our candidate is, admittedly, a bit higher than The Home Depot. However, it's not materially higher. Furthermore, its net leverage ratio is lower than competitor Floor & Decor Holdings Inc. (FND). When it comes to margins, Lowe's Companies is a bit lower than The Home Depot, as the chart below illustrates. However, it is still quite a bit better than what Floor & Decor Holdings Inc. boasts.

When it comes to 2025, there is a great deal of uncertainty at this moment. Prior to this whole tariff situation coming into play, it was expected that repair and remodeling activity would be up this year, with spending by the end of 2025 hitting $509 billion compared to the $503 billion it was in 2024. With tariffs now in place, there is the risk that spending could ultimately be lower. According to one estimate, about $204 billion worth of goods were used in the construction of both new multifamily and single family houses in 2024. $14 billion of these goods were imported.

It stands to reason that any increase on goods for home building will also apply to the goods that are also used on the repair and remodeling of homes. Higher prices will deter consumer spending, especially when we consider that these price increases will likely affect other aspects of the economy also. As a result of this, investors should certainly be watching the situation closely.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I can understand why some investors might be a bit worried about Lowe's Companies. There certainly is uncertainty when it comes to tariffs. But if we focus solely on the fundamentals and the data that is currently available at the moment, this is a quality company that might not be as attractive as The Home Depot, but that is trading at a pretty meaningful discount to it. Its net leverage ratio is not out of control by any means, and management has a good track record of returning capital to shareholders. Given these factors, I believe that upgrading the stock to a soft “buy” is appropriate at this time.

data.title: Workiva: A Great Buy On Recent Weakness
data.description:
Over the past month, the market has been driven primarily by fear. Investors rushed out of stocks and clamored to buy international stocks and gold as a bulwark against President Trump's rising trade war. And while I agree with the diversification moves, I also believe we should deploy careful stock-picking to acquire shares in companies that have been whacked in this correction, despite strong fundamentals.

Workiva (NYSE:WK) is one such name. The financial reporting stock has an unfortunate association with the ESG movement, causing its stock to drop ~30% since the start of January. Though the ESG backlash is certainly real, it's not a meaningful enough portion of Workiva's business to cause a growth slowdown: in fact, Workiva posted accelerating growth in its most recent quarter.

I last wrote a bullish note on Workiva in December, when the stock was still trading above $100 per share. At the time, I had championed the company's strong growth, and in particular its high net revenue retention rates in a tough climate for other enterprise software peers. I had cautioned on Workiva's burgeoning valuation in the $100s, but this problem vanishes when the stock is currently sitting in the mid-$70s. With a robust growth outlook for FY 2025, I'm reiterating my buy rating for Workiva.

One of the biggest reasons that Workiva has been under pressure is that the company has a big association with the rise and fall of the ESG fad. And yes; while it’s true that ESG initiatives drove a lot of the company’s revenue growth during the pandemic era, it’s a small chunk of the company’s addressable market today. The chart below, taken from Workiva’s latest Q4 earnings presentation, showcases that the company believes it has a massive $35 billion global TAM, into which its current ~$0.9 billion revenue run rate is only ~3% penetrated. And though Workiva doesn’t provide exact breakouts, we can see that the sustainability/management portion of the company’s market is represented by just the small dark green sliver (which is proportionally higher, but only marginally so, in Europe which has more complex environmental requirements than other markets):

Furthermore, we note that in spite of the plethora of headlines of companies (as well as universities) dismantling their ESG and diversity initiatives in order to align more closely to the Trump 2.0 administration, Workiva has posted consistent growth rates that even accelerated in the most recent quarter. In other words, despite the ongoing political shifts that are slicing jobs out of ESG departments, Workiva has a thriving business in everyday humdrum corporate and financial reporting that is more than offsetting any ESG product declines.

Amid pessimism for the name, however, Workiva has slid to very appealing valuations. At current share prices near $73, Workiva trades at a market cap of $4.10 billion. After we net off the $816.4 million of cash and $764.9 million of debt from the latest balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $4.05 billion.

Meanwhile, for the upcoming fiscal year FY 2025, Workiva has guided to $864-$868 million in revenue, or a 17% y/y growth rate. It’s also calling for 5.0-5.5% in pro forma operating margins, which is a 120bps lift from FY 2024.

This positions Workiva’s valuation at just 4.7x EV/FY 2025 revenue. Now, we note that a number of the most prominent names in the software sector like Salesforce (CRM) and Workday (WDAY), which I consider to be the bellwethers for the sector in terms of valuation, are also expected to grow in FY 2025 at a mid-low teens pace (in other words, slightly slower than Workiva). And yet, Workiva trades at the more discounted multiple:

In my view, the markets have unfairly punished Workiva for its association to the ESG trend, though there is absolutely no evidence that companies’ retreat from ESG is actually causing growth headwinds for Workiva. Amid that dislocation, it’s a great time for patient investors to dive into this name.

Q4 Download

Let’s now go through Workiva’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Workiva’s revenue grew 20% y/y to $199.9 million, ahead of Wall Street’s expectations of $195.2 million (+17% y/y) by a three point margin. And as previously mentioned, in spite of ESG fears, the company actually achieved accelerating revenue growth; accelerating three points sequentially versus 17% growth in Q3.

Adding to the optimism, we note that Workiva’s net retention rates have been climbing as well: in Q4, hitting a 112% rate, indicating that the average Workiva customer is boosting their spend on the platform by 12%.

This makes Workiva a relative outlier in the software sector, given that many companies are reporting the opposite: declining net retention rates, as corporate efficiency measures lead to headcount trimming that is impacting seat-based software products. Again, Workiva’s relative strength is running counter to the narrative that the disenfranchisement of ESG is having an impact on its results.

A couple of factors are contributing to this outperformance. First, the company is selling more multi-product deals, especially as it continues to target larger enterprise clientele. Second - just because the U.S. is eschewing ESG doesn’t mean other markets are. A large chunk of Workiva’s core customers and multinational giants, which have to comply with overseas regulations. The company noted that the EU has recently released new complex reporting requirements called the Omnibus Simplification Package, compliance to which is driving new sales for Workiva’s reporting compliance solutions.

Per CEO Julie Iskow’s remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Our Q4 results are supported by strong underlying business fundamentals. We exceeded expectations in our account expansion activity, showcased by our net retention rate improving to 112%. Our large contract value customers continue to accelerate as well.

The number of contracts valued over $300,000 increased 34%, and those over $500,000 increased 32%, all compared to Q4 of 2023. Whether from new logos or account expansion, we're encouraged by our win rates, our increasing deal sizes and the multi-solution platform demand from our customers (…)

While we credit our sustainability solutions with driving part of our 2024 growth acceleration, our financial reporting solutions remain the primary revenue driver for the business. We saw broad-based demand in our financial reporting solution set, including SEC reporting, multi-entity reporting, private company reporting and management reporting. We also have been particularly successful in driving high-value deals with financial services firms with our industry-specific solutions, which include fund reporting for investment firms, regulatory reporting for insurance companies and risk and compliance solutions for banks.”

Workiva’s outlook calling for high-teens growth in FY 2025 after an already strong FY 2024, in my view, is a confirmation of the company’s confidence in its ability to overcome any sustainability related headwinds and keep pushing its core product sales forward.

It’s worth noting as well that Workiva is beginning to generate meaningful cash flow. In FY 2024, the company hit $86.3 million of FCF, up 25% y/y:

Given the company’s expectations for operating margin expansion next year, we expect FCF to grow at a faster pace than revenue as well in FY 2025.

Risks and Key Takeaways

In my view, fears of an ESG slowdown are unfounded for Workiva, as demonstrated by the company’s accelerating growth rates and healthy net expansion trends. Of course, that’s not to say that risks aren’t present for the company. In particular, macroeconomic uncertainty and the recent malaise brought on my Trump’s escalating tariff wars may push many companies to slow or delay their plans for international expansion, and/or pause their implantation of complex software products like Workiva.

That being said, I do believe that risk is already more than captured into this stock’s revenue multiple at less than 5x. Take advantage of this dip to buy with confidence.

data.title: Intuit: Prime Candidate For An Activist Investor To Jump In
data.description:
Intuit (INTU) is facing multiple risks, including tax filing alternatives, tax cuts, and a tougher economic environment putting pressure on small and medium businesses, the company's most significant customer cohort.

The company's margins remain far below pre-pandemic levels, making it an attractive play for an activist investor, in my opinion.

Still, the stock trades at a lofty valuation, especially when looking at real earnings and real free cash flows.

Let's dive in.

Introduction To Intuit

Intuit operates across four business segments, in Global Business Solutions, Consumer, Credit Karma, and ProTax.

Under Global Business Solutions, Intuit provides a full suite of solutions to small and medium businesses across business and financial management, payroll, payments, marketing and more. This segment is responsible for 59% of the company's revenues and about 60% of its segment operating income.

Under Consumer, Intuit provides the TurboTax service for DIY tax filing. This segment represents 27% of Intuit's sales, and 30% of its segment operating profit. In recent years, there have been talks about potential government-provided alternatives to TurboTax, including one developed by DOGE. So far, these haven't materialized into a real threat.

Credit Karma is a platform that helps individuals manage their finances and make smart credit and buying decisions in things like credit cards, home, auto or personal loans. Credit Karma represents 10% of company revenues and about 4% of segment profit.

Lastly, ProTax, serves professional accountants, who typically work with larger tax filers. ProTax is a small part of the group, responsible for 6% of sales and 5% of operating profit.

Since I started covering Intuit with a 'Sell' rating, shares have underperformed the market. In addition, over the past three years, Intuit was roughly in line with the market.

To me, there are three reasons for the stock's poor performance. First, it was and still remains extremely richly valued when looking at comparable metrics rather than the Non-GAAP adjusted ones the company provides. Second, risks are increasing. Third, and probably related to the Non-GAAP issue, Intuit was quite passive in improving efficiencies and profitability, unlike many other software peers.

Intuit Didn't Exploit The 2022 Sell Off To Get Lean

Intuit belongs to a group of companies that saw their shares plunge in the 2022 selloff.

Many of these companies aggressively pushed for growth, regardless of profitability, and when the market changed its tone and interest rates began to rise, their stocks dropped sharply.

In Intuit's case, its operating margins dropped from 28% in 2020 to 20% in 2022, while its employee count jumped from about 10,000 to over 17,000.

All the peers in the graph above, including Meta (META), Wix (WIX), and Shopify (SHOP), exploited the wake-up call, changed their culture completely, and went through extensive layoffs. All of them are now demonstrating peak profitability and accelerated execution with the sole outlier being Intuit.

Intuit said it will reduce stock-based compensation over time, and focus on margin expansion. However, this hasn't really come to fruition. As revenue growth dropped from 32% in 2022 to about 13% in 2023 and 2024, operating margins increased by an underwhelming 200 bps, and remain far below pre-pandemic and pre-acquisition levels. Meanwhile, SBC as a percentage of sales is holding firm at 12%.

Meanwhile, free cash flow margins, adjusted for SBC, were 17% in 2024, the lowest level since at least 2017, and the employee count continues to rise, now at 18,800.

The way I see it, Intuit has significant room to drive efficiencies, and the reason they are not going for it is because they are aggressively trying to diversify away from the tax business, in a sign of worry about potential risks. Still, I think that just like it worked with Wix, an activist investor could shake things up with Intuit.

What Do Intuit's Results & Guidance Tell Us?

In the second quarter of its fiscal 2025, Intuit generated revenues of about $4.0 billion, slightly better than estimates, with 17% growth. Operating profit was close to $600 million, reflecting a 410 bps improvement in margin, driven by better gross margins and operational leverage.

The Global Business Solutions segment achieved revenue growth of 19% to reach $2.7 billion, led by their Online ecosystem, which grew 21%, driven by customer growth and higher revenue per customer. Segment operating profit improved by 220 bps.

Credit Karma revenues were up 36%, driven by credit card, personal loan, and auto insurance offerings. Operating margin improved 16 points, as operational leverage is kicking in, though margins declined from the previous quarter.

For Consumer and ProTax, the second quarter is borderline meaningless, as the tax season begins in the company's fiscal Q3.

Interestingly, despite coming way ahead of guidance in both Global Business and Credit Karma growth, Intuit only reaffirmed its guidance, rather than raising it.

Similarly, guidance across all profit metrics remained the same, which means Intuit expects roughly 18%-20% EPS growth in 2024, driven by a 330-350 bps improvement in GAAP operating margin.

Intuit's Valuation Remains Lofty

At $599 a share, Intuit trades at 48 times forward GAAP EPS, based on the high end of the company's guidance. Assuming it will grow earnings by an additional 18% next year, it trades at 40 times FY26 earnings.

This puts Intuit at roughly a 65% premium over Microsoft (MSFT), another business-facing software company, with similar growth expectations and I'd argue, lower risks.

If we use the adjusted metrics Intuit provides, which primarily exclude stock-based compensation expense, we can compare it to other companies that are doing a similar trick.

Keep in mind that the higher the company's SBC is, the lower its adjusted multiple becomes, which makes the following graph almost irrelevant in my eyes.

Adobe (ADBE), Salesforce (CRM), and ServiceNow (NOW), are all business-facing software companies, though they slightly differ in size from Intuit's core cohorts. Still, as we can see, Intuit is trading at a 30% premium over Salesforce, which is expected to grow slightly slower, but is a lower risk subscription-based business. It's also trading at close to a 60% premium over Adobe.

The only comp that's trading above Intuit is ServiceNow, which is expected to grow revenues at a high-teens CAGR for the foreseeable future, compared to Intuit's 12%, and generates the majority of its revenues from recurring subscriptions.

To me, Intuit should trade in the range of 30x-35x its real GAAP earnings. This would still give it a meaningful premium over Microsoft, but means it's about 20% overvalued.

Conclusion

Intuit is a strong and differentiated software company, with a declining reliance on its tax business, making it a more resilient company in the face of upcoming risks.

The company's underwhelming profitability and inflated stock-based compensation expenses make it an attractive activist play, as there seems to be significant room to drive efficiencies and cut costs.

Until then, Intuit continues to trade at an extremely rich valuation of 48 times forward earnings, when looking at real profits.

This makes Intuit a 'Sell.'

data.title: Managing Expectations For A Global Trade War
data.description:
The sweeping policy change for US tariffs announced yesterday by President Trump is a game changer for the global economy. Exactly how trade flows shift, economies pivot and governments around the world react is unclear, but most forecasts point to slower growth, higher inflation and a reduction in global trade. Trump, by contrast, says the tariffs will “quickly” usher in a new era of prosperity for the US.

This much is clear: a grand experiment in global trade, with trillions of dollars in economic activity at stake, is underway. As with any policy shift, the results are unknown. It will take several months at a minimum to assess the impact.

President Trump, of course, says the outlook is bright. Speaking yesterday at the White House, where he outlined the new tariff policy, he said: “For years, hard working American citizens were forced to sit on the sidelines as other nations got rich and powerful, much of it at our expense. But now it’s our turn to prosper, and in so doing, use trillions and trillions of dollars to reduce our taxes and pay down our national debt, and it’ll all happen very quickly.”

Many analysts disagree, including Steven Blitz, chief US economist at GlobalData TS Lombard. “They just announced a major tax hike, mostly on the corporate side, but as with most corporate taxes, they will be translated into higher prices to the consumer. And you don’t grow an economy with higher taxes,” he says.

The odds of recession are rising as tariffs increase, according to a growing number of estimates. The Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model is currently nowcasting that the US economy has already started contracting in the first quarter (see chart below). But other forecasts offer a brighter profile, at least at the moment. The New York Fed’s model, for example, is still nowcasting a solid 2.9% increase for Q1 GDP.

But any optimism may be soon fade, forecasts Olu Sonola, the head of US Economic Research at Fitch Ratings. “Many countries will likely end up in a recession. You can throw most forecasts out the door if this tariff rate stays on for an extended period of time.”

Professor Niven Winchester, a researcher at MIT’s Program on the Science and Policy of Global Change, lays out a new set of economic forecasts that show significant economic hits across the board, based on modeling that assumes countries will respond with tariff increases – a likely scenario, given the announcements from around the world in the past 24 hours.

Some observers can argue that short-term pain for long-term gain may be worthwhile when assessing the tradeoffs for Trump’s tariffs. But there’s an inherent conflict in the White House’s tariff policy, notes St. Joseph’s University economics professor Nancy Ruth Fox. If the goal of the policy shift is to raise revenue for the US via imports, that objective is undermined by increasing America’s industrial base. “If I continue buying goods that are produced abroad, that will bring in tariff revenue. But it won’t do anything for American production, not anything, but just at a very simple level,” she explains. Meanwhile, if American consumers switch to buying domestic goods, tariff revenue declines.

Note, too, that for all the talk of the US trade deficit, which drives much of Trump’s thinking on trade policy, America has long enjoyed a trade surplus, and one that’s growing. Indeed, the US is the leading exporter of services to the world, such as financial services and cloud computing. The US trade surplus in services was nearly $300 billion in 2024.

The question is whether the tariffs threaten US services exports? The answer is almost certainly “yes,” at least in some degree. Given that many services areas represent growth industries, the price tag from tariffs could be quite painful for the US.

Foreign governments may target US services in a tit-for-tat trade war. “The European Union is now equipped with policy tools to extend the range of retaliation against US tariffs to target imports of US services,” advises Filippo Taddei, a managing director of global investment research at Goldman Sachs.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Village Super Market: Still On A Tear, And Why It May (Or May Not) Continue
data.description:
2025 may be only one-quarter complete, but thus far, my "on the fence" thesis regarding Village Super Market, Inc. (NASDAQ:VLGEA) has been proven wrong. Those bullish on VLGEA stock have been tap dancing all the way to the bank.

Shares in the New Jersey-based operator of ShopRite and Fairway grocery stores have continued to perform well. Even as the stock has briefly been affected by market volatility, VLGEA is up by double-digits year to date.

For comparison, the broad market, as measured by the S&P 500 (SP500), is in the red. The Russell 2000 (RTY), a benchmark for small-cap stocks, is down by an even greater degree. Besides the obvious point that Village Super Market, as a defensive name with all its operations in the United States, is far less impacted by recent macroeconomic developments, another factor driving this stock's continued strong performance has been fiscal performance.

Namely, a continued resurgence in earnings growth. While I was initially skeptical about VLGEA continuing to make new multi-year highs, if this trend continues, a further re-rating may be in store. That said, while VLGEA remains cheap, don't rule out the possibility that shares eventually cough back a large portion of these recently accrued gains.

VLGEA Stock: Recap and Recent News

As mentioned above, right at the start of the year, I laid out the argument as to why VLGEA, despite its shift from sleepy underperformer to rally recipient, should be approached cautiously.

In a nutshell, I pointed out that the prospect of lower interest rates, not just a re-acceleration in earnings growth, was key to VLGEA's more than 20% surge during 2025.

Moreover, while conceding that the stock, when considering the underlying value of its assets, could rally by another 20% before reaching fair value, I argued that, due to the ownership structure of this family-controlled company, and its likely further negative impact on dividend growth, additional upside may be limited.

Flash forward to now. VLGEA, closing out 2024 in the low-$30s per share, is now in the mid-to-high $30s per share, and not too far off from my "fair value" price target of $38.60 per share. While investors briefly threw out this stock with the bathwater during the February sell-off, and as of this writing appear to be doing so in response to the tariff-driven sell-off, market fear and uncertainty has been no match for the continued reporting of strong quarterly results.

Pre-market on March 4, 2025, Village Super Market reported earnings for the fiscal quarter ending Jan. 25, 2025. During the quarter, overall sales were up 4% year-over-year, with same store sales increasing 2.3% compared to the prior year's quarter.

More importantly, net income was up 17.3% year-over-year, with EPS of $1.14 coming in 17.5% above the 97 cents in EPS reported for Q2 FY2024. As I discussed in my prior VLGEA write-up, a large factor in earnings growth has been the positive impact of Village either updating or replacing older stores with new, modernized locations. The most recent of these store replacements, in Old Bridge, New Jersey, was completed last fiscal year.

In addition, this old school grocery operator, a member of the Wakefern grocery cooperative, has also more strongly embraced online ordering and other 21st century innovations to the grocery shopping experience.

As store modernization and digital ordering has played a material role in Village's re-acceleration of earnings growth, there may be the concern that this is a "one and done" phenomenon, and that growth in future quarters may decelerate.

However, as also mentioned previously, Village Super Market has continued with its store replacement strategy this fiscal year. In the latest 10-Q filing, Village Super Market stated that it has budgeted $75 million in capital expenditures during FY 2025, with much of this figure being spent on replacement store locations in East Orange, NJ and Watchung, NJ.

If this translates into similarly sized earnings growth in FY2026 as it has in FY2025, VLGEA stock may not only be poised to gain in tandem with earnings growth, but could experience additional multiple expansion.

Village Super Market Valuation

Previously, I valued Village Super Market in a way that accounted for the fact that, despite the company holding numerous "hidden assets," the likelihood of Village monetizing the majority of these assets was very low, given how important they are to the company's operations and profitability.

In other words, while VLGEA may own real estate valued on the books for $154.9 million, and hold interests in real estate partnership valued at $20 million, I just don't see a family-run company doing a sale/leaseback or some other sort of financial engineering, in order to gin up a quick gain for shares.

As for Village's other key asset, its membership interest in Wakefern, it's not as if the company can redeem its interest for its full market value, then proceed with a "go it alone" strategy, without severely affecting operations.

That said, we can continue to take into account one particular "hidden asset" of VLGEA: the total value of notes receivable, representing funds lent by the company to Wakefern. As of January 25, 2025, these notes receivable were worth around $107.2 million.

Currently, VLGEA has a market cap of around $553.7 million. Add in debt and capital leases totaling $358.6 million, and deducting a nearly $133.9 million cash position, and we get an enterprise value for VLGEA of around $778.4 million.

Deducting the notes receivable from Wakefern, and we get an adjusted enterprise value of $671.2 million. With EBITDA over the trailing twelve-month period coming in at around $105.4 million, the company today is valued at an adjusted TTM EV/EBITDA ratio of 6.4.

Compare that to the valuation of most other grocery store stocks. Albertsons (ACI), for instance, trades at a TTM EBITDA multiple of around 7x. Weis Markets (WMK) trades at a TTM EBITDA multiple of 7.8x. Kroger's (KR) EBITDA multiple, at 8.3x, is even higher, with another grocery chain that's turned into a growth story, Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM) trading at a TTM EBITDA multiple of 26.4x!

Now, I don't think that Village is on the verge of turning into a Sprouts Farmers Market-style situation. On the other hand, given its return to growth, coupled with the company's stronger balance sheet relative to other legacy grocery operators like Albertsons and Kroger, I believe that shares could sustain a higher valuation, assuming earnings growth from store replacements continue.

Valuing VLGEA at the upper end of valuations for other legacy grocery stores (8x-9x), we get a gross valuation of between $843.2 million and $948.6 million for the company, based on TTM EBITDA.

Subtract debt, and add back cash and the notes receivable, we get a net valuation for VLGEA of between $725.7 million and $831.1 million, or between $49.20 and $56.35 per share, between 31.9% and 51% above current prices.

Based upon TTM earnings, a move to such price levels would represent a re-rating of VLGEA to a P/E ratio in the mid-teens range, reasonable when compared to the TTM P/E ratios of other grocery store stocks.

The Case for (and Against) a Continued VLGEA Rally

Admittedly, after underestimating VLGEA's potential to re-rate based on earnings growth improvement, it may seem like I'm flip-flopping to a position that the VLGEA rally has far more room to run.

However, given that, following the latest earnings release, and the market's reaction to them, it's becoming more clear that the growth trend driven by store replacements could keep this stock on an upward trajectory.

Remember, my valuation analysis above is based on TTM results, not results in the quarters ahead. Additional earnings growth resulting from replacement store openings may mean VLGEA rallies to the high-$40s/low-$50s per share, yet remains at a valuation on par or slightly below that of its larger peers.

In this scenario, dividend growth, or the lack thereof, likely will not matter. That said, it's not set in stone that an earnings growth resurgence, driven by replacement store openings, will persist, this fiscal year and beyond.

Village Super Market reported yet another quarter of double-digit year-over-year earnings growth, largely due to the fact that the Old Bridge, NJ replacement store wasn't open during the quarter ending Jan. 25, 2024.

The above-mentioned replacement store in Watchung, NJ is scheduled to open on April 13, but that may not be in time to have much impact on results for the current fiscal quarter.

The Crossings at Brick Church Station, the real estate development where the East Orange, NJ replacement store will be located, continues to have a 2025 completion date, but it's unclear whether that means completion is imminent, or if it will occur during the latter months of the calendar year.

Hence, the potential positive impact on earnings may be more than a fiscal quarter away. In turn, if year-over-year growth for VLGEA during Q3 FY 2025 is less impressive than growth in the recently completed quarter, this could have an impact on VLGEA's price performance in the near-term.

Not only that, this is assuming that the new stores, even if they open more than a quarter from now, will lead to similarly sized improvements to earnings as the FY2024 replacement store openings.

Best-case scenario, this could simply result in VLGEA staying stuck at or near current price levels, a positive development for existing shareholders but not a great prospect for those mulling a purchase today.

Worst-case scenario, if subsequent results prove that Village's earnings growth during the preceding fiscal year was truly a "one and done" situation, VLGEA could decline in price, even if earnings stay near current levels.

Shares may seem cheap at 10.4x TTM earnings, but remember that this stock has at times traded for as low as 7.7 times earnings in the recent past. Based on TTM earnings, if profitability stagnates again, and shares derate to such a low valuation, this mid-$30s stock will be back down in the high-$20s per share.

Bottom Line on Village Super Market

When it comes to other possible catalysts with Village Super Market, I remain skeptical. Although last year's passing of Sumas family patriarch William P. Sumas suggests that VLGEA's founding family could be more interested in selling the business, it's not as if another grocery chain could easily buy Village, given how its Wakefern membership is integral to its operations.

Barring some scenario where a private equity firm starts buying up Wakefern members, in an effort to turn this retail cooperative into a single consolidated company, the only logical buyer for VLGEA is Saker ShopRite, the only Wakefern member larger than Village.

I also continue to be skeptical about future dividend increases, given the current dual-class share structure. Then again, perhaps the Sumas family will decide to take advantage of the full convertibility of Class B shares into Class A shares, enabling them to either sell them into the public market, or to benefit from higher dividends.

Nevertheless, with VLGEA stock, continue to view earnings growth as the primary catalyst for shares going forward. If the replacement store strategy continues to pay off, substantial upside could remain.

While there is the risk of a lag time between now and when these soon-to-open replacement stores positively affect earnings, this may work in your favor. With shares pulling back due to the latest broad market sell-off, the opportunity to accumulate a position in the low-$30s per share may be imminent.

data.title: Esquire Financial Holdings: Still Bullish
data.description:
Back in early December of last year, one company that I came close to downgrading was Esquire Financial Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:ESQ). For those not familiar with it, it is a rather small financial institution with a market capitalization as of this writing of $598.7 million. This would not have been the first time that I downgraded the company. That occurred in September of last year after the stock had risen significantly. Because of this, I ended up downgrading the stock from a ‘strong buy’ to a ‘buy’. This wasn't because of fundamental weakness or anything like that. Rather, it was because of how expensive the stock had become.

From the time I first rated the company a ‘strong buy’ back in June 2024 through the present day, shares have risen by 63.8%. That's 10 times better than the 6.3% increase achieved by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. However, since my most recent article in December, the stock has pulled back by 2.7%. The good news is that this still outperforms the broader market, which has seen a 5.2% drop over the same window time. This pullback is not enough to change my opinion that the firm is pretty close to deserving a downgrade. But with the most recent financial data coming out that showed continued growth of the enterprise, I'm not ready to pull the trigger on that just yet.

A look at recent data

Unlike your traditional bank, Esquire Financial Holdings has a very specific focus. The company prioritizes catering to the litigation industry. Its operations do this on a national scale and achieve it without having any branches whatsoever. This is because, instead of focusing on a physical footprint, Esquire Financial Holdings focus is on technologies that make it highly scalable. Of course, the institution does not provide only banking services. It also offers other services as well, such as payment processing activities. In fact, its Independent Sales Organization model has empowered it to reach 88,000 merchants across the US.

The most recent data that we have regarding the company covers the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. During this time, net interest income was $25.2 million. That's up 18.9% compared to the $21.2 million reported one year earlier. This is in spite of the fact that the company's net interest margin dropped from 6.12% to 5.87%. The growth, then, was really attributable to an expansion of the firm's balance sheet. But that is something I will get to shortly. It is worth noting that the final quarter of 2024 was not the only time that net interest income increased. For 2024 as a whole, the $95.2 million generated by the company was well above the $79.2 million reported the previous year. For this window of time, the company's net interest margin contracted as well, but only marginally from 6.09% to 6.06%.

The business also saw some changes when it came to its non-interest income. But these changes, unfortunately, were for the worst. Year over year for the final quarter, this metric fell slightly from $6.3 million to $6.2 million. During this window of time, payment processing fees contracted from $5.4 million to $5.1 million. And for the year as a whole, non-interest income dropped from $29.8 million to $24.9 million. Part of this decline was because, in 2023, Esquire Financial Holdings booked a $4 million gain on equity securities. However, the company also saw a contraction in payment processing fees from $22.3 million to $20.9 million.

This weakness was driven by a decline in transactions and processing volume. As an example, for the final quarter of the year, the company solved volumes hit $9.2 billion. That was down 8.6% year over year. The number of payment transactions dropped 6.5% year over year from 155.8 million to 145.7 million. Management blamed all of this on ISO and merchant attrition and changes in volumes associated with its overall merchant risk profile. Regardless of the cause, investors should certainly keep an eye on these changes moving forward. Even with this bit of weakness, net profits improved in the final quarter to $11.8 million compared to the $9.9 million reported the previous year. And for the year as a whole, the $43.7 million in profit generated by the company came in above the $41 million reported the prior year.

When it comes to the balance sheet, we have seen continued growth. The company ended 2024 with deposits of $1.64 billion. This compares nicely to the $1.54 billion reported for the third quarter of 2024 and is up from the $1.41 billion reported at the end of 2023. As the chart above illustrates, deposits for the institution have risen nicely over the past few years. The primary growth driver during this time has been NOW deposits. These are a highly desirable type of deposit. I say this because they carry some of the lowest costs to them. To put this in perspective, during the final quarter of the year, savings, NOW, and money market deposits cost the company only 1.37% in interest per annum. That compares to the 4.71% for time deposits. And it was also significantly better than the 9.04% that took on debt costs.

This has not been the only area of growth for the institution. We also have loans. During 2024, loans totaled $1.38 billion. That's up from the $1.28 billion reported a single quarter earlier and was up from the $1.19 billion reported at the end of 2023. Unlike any other financial institution that I have seen, the lion’s share of these loans are dedicated to the litigation space. About 60% of its loan portfolio, or $836 million, involves commercial litigation causes. A distant second place belongs to the multifamily category at 26%, or $355 million. As the image below illustrates, most of the litigation loans are focused on working capital needs.

But the company does have a lot of exposure to case disbursement activities. Furthermore, these loans are heavily diversified based on geographic area, with California, New York, Texas, and Florida, comprising the largest chunks. Considering that these four states are essentially the economic engine of the United States and major centers for the practice of law, this is not surprising to me in the least bit. I have also been encouraged by the fact that management has continued to carry no debt on the company's books as of the end of each fiscal quarter. This means that it is a low-risk prospect with very little probability of solvency issues.

Unfortunately, shares of the business are far from cheap. In the chart above, you can see how the stock is valued on a price-to-earnings basis. With a price-to-earnings multiple of 13.7, it's quite a bit above the 6 to 10 range that I like to see in this industry. However, in that chart, you can also see how it stacks up against five similar firms. Three of the five are cheaper than it is, while the other two are more expensive. This place is the company around the middle of the group from a valuation standpoint. In the chart below, you can also see its price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples stacked up against the same five businesses. In this regard, the firm is very expensive, with its stock trading at levels well above many of the other similar enterprises. This would normally be a deal-breaker for me. But as you will see shortly, the company boasts high asset quality. This comes as a result of a large portion of its revenue coming from non-interest activities and because of the high net interest margin that it can capture because of its emphasis on the litigation space.

In the first chart below, you can see the return on assets for Esquire Financial Holdings. With a reading of 2.49%, it is leagues above the competition.
